As wind is highly variable using gears could also add significant complexity and breakdown potential so if possible direct drive is preferable.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text:
The rule of thumb I go by is this ...
Big gear driving little gear; High Torque and Low RPM ---> Low Torque and High RPM. 
Small gear driving big gear;  Low Torque and High RPM ---> High Torque and Low RPM.
Power isn't involved.

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Funny. You are reading my mind. I solved this type of problem for a company. They had an educational hydrogenator simulator. They were trying to run the generator directly off of the turbine blade. It would never run successfully in the minimal current they had available. I added a large gear and small gear and applied the torque to the generator. I'm also looking into toy gear boxes. 3D printer fun. You'd think cheep speed reduction gear boxes were available, nope. Another study for the amateur: Wheel Train in clocks. Ever wonder how to make a 1 RMP motor? "Nerd Alert." This study will lead you back to the development of the pocket watch. That's some serious English history. Thank you for this video Dr. Smith.

author: @kadmow
text:
good you got to  power at 3:25 - not sure going straight to wind turbines clarifies - seeiing as real wind turbines do often have gears, using  V-belts for alternator coupling would truly be a dumb move if one were being "output power" obsessed/  

Of course a direct drive 5MW generator may be impractical, while a geared unit is quite compact and able to handle hurricanes perched 120-200m up in the air..

Stating / Defiining up front what is power could be useful....  Power  = Force x Speed, =Torque x Rotational-speed, will bring the speed / torque argument around knowing that  for a given Energy rate, Power is constant...  (Power also = Work over Time , how that confuses some lay folk - seeing how all those powers are the same...  Time rate of energy.)  - some "funny dude"" online piqued my funny bone by stating that Torque IS power...    I was amused at their response to "facts" - he/she/it  was just a fanboi/non-bin of "popular ideology" with no advanced understanding...   cheers...


Output power = Input power x "efficiency factor" (ie. efficiency is always less than 1  - until over-unity arrives  and Thermo2 becomes optional) speed and torque are related - choose one the other will be thus defined.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: Here’s a good reason to use gears. Say you want to design an electric car, you could have a motor with enough torque so that it can be coupled directly to the the differential but such a motor would draw too much current and be too heavy. Alternatively you could have a high speed low torque motor of the same power that is only a fraction of the weight and only draws a fraction of the current at a similar voltage. In this case you will need a gear reduction to make some torque unfortunately this cannot be taken too extremes because of the braking effect of such a great gear reduction as well as the noise generated. There maybe a solution though, magnetic gears on a spline which are only engaged while the motor is driving would resolve the braking problem and be completely quiet all the time and never wear out.

author: @jmir33
text: I'd like to just mention that commercial scale turbines need to output 60Hz AC electricity so have gearing not just to overcome friction and the like, but also to maintain that frequency of AC output. When you consider the fact that the wind speed varies a lot, it's incredible that they're able to seamlessly integrate in the grid.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: or use an inverter  (variable blade pitch does more than just look good - feathered in the wind.  Variable pitch is another form of gearing - coupling the rotor to the windspeed - if within attainable limits...  Another gearing form is control of electro-magnet field strength,  a control level that gets left out with simple series or permanent magnet generators - only possible on shunt or hybrid shunt wound architecture..) for control simplicity just go PM, rectify and output through an inverter...  lol.

author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
hydraulics = mega losses, the end.  - or is there a real hidden angle - the fluid can cool the generator, allowing increased power density, yes it can provide remote location of many systems - but so could a shaft inside the tower - or a chain/belt...  One has to overcome pumping losses  etc.

Next -  Air with heat recovery...  These others may have direct storage implications, but are geographically limited whereas the power grid is "less so". -)

The current paradigm often does involve pumping and many wind turbines driving one giant water pump- for pumped hydro, though evaporation and the hydrology cycle gets more done, most of the time.

author: @rjstewart
text: Not related to this current video but you've inspired me to do some experimentation.   I've been doing some experimentation using carbon felt as a wick in Crisco vegetable shortening.  In one I used a piece of wire to keep the wick upright and in another I sandwiched it between some stainless steel mesh with about .25 cm sticking out. The wick and holder design isn't that interesting but I'm now going to remake the shortening emergency candles I have here but using carbon felt for my wick!

author: @MACLADILLY
text: Good video. I had a revelation not so long ago where I realised that Torque = amps and Speed = voltage as regards rotary generation. I was pedalling my bicycle DC 12v generator (using a treadmill motor as generator). As I reached the battery bank voltage, the effort required to push the pedals suddenly increased a lot! I felt like I had reached a threshold. Suddenly I was pushing amps into the batteries by hard pedalling. A good example if learning through doing rather than by theory. Also, fast pedalling = higher voltage.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
Did you also note that input power (mechanical) was limited (lim Torque x RPM (rad.sec) )   and as Electrically Power = IV (Current x "EMF" electro motive force = (Amps x Volts) - correlations exist - also the resistance of the generator winding alters both as R = V/I (ohms law).   
Many ways to achieve desired outcome - or skin cats..

author: @Xero1of1
text: I have a question. Higher torque means you can turn a much heavier object... If you had a larger generator in size but generates at the same velocity as the turbine (low voltage high current?) could you not then step up that voltage (reducing the current) to something usable using a transformer? You wouldn't have to worry about keeping a gearbox cool and lubricated and you could transmit the power generated immediately down the turbine via wires to a transformer on the ground. The transformers we put on poles are filled with vegetable oil and can sit up there for 50+ years without ever having to perform maintenance on them because there aren't any moving parts... (I'm a designer for a power company). I guess I'm wondering we we choose to go with the more mechanical route versus the more electrical route for changing mechanical motion into electrical power on the large wind turbines.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text:
my comment is from a fair bit of ignorance, but my understanding it that the turbine is geared up to rotate the generators faster than the blades to get higher voltage for two reasons: higher voltage is easier to store/use. secondly in alternating currents it transfers longer distances with less loss.

i.e. high amperage low voltage is comparably useless, in DC you cannot even charge the batteries below 12.75 volts, and in AC you cannot transmit the power far enough for a useful grid.

replies.author: @tichrontus
replies.text:
Not sure what exactly you're looking at but I'll throw out some stuff that I know and you see if that answers your questions...

Putting more focus on improving the mechanical aspect is an option, except that it does not work in the way by attempting to maximize its torque to draw out max useable power. Conversion of mechanical energy is similar to dealing with circuits that operate around resonant frequencies (I assume you're of electrical background). What i have read before, such type of circuits are able to achieve max real power at the resonant frequency which is affected by the total inductance and capacitance. Operating above or below that frequency will resulting in attenuation of real power. Similarly, conversion of mechanical energy has what's called a power curve which is a function of the torque and rpm. Ideally, operating the turbine at the peak of the power curve gives the most converted power. Except that the winds do not exactly cooperate with us. The challenge when setting up wind turbines is that you need to know wind conditions in the area, derive the rpm output range after considering the blade profile, find out the power curve which is usually dependant on the specs of the alternator used and decide the gearbox ratio to align with the power curve.

What I threw out above is purely based on memory so you'll have to do your own research as well, in case my memory did fail me. Hope that helps!

replies.author: @noaholiverpang5250
replies.text: (Coming from a high school student so I could be very wrong lol) I believe your idea has merit in the sense that using a transformer to step up the voltage would be more efficient and require less maintenance. The only issue I would say is that you would need a really large direct drive generator and larger generator —> more expensive. Also at that point you could simply reduce the gauge of wire in the generator to increase the voltage there without the need for a transformer. Your idea would work great if someone found a way to make a compact low voltage high current generator though.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: actually noah brought up a pretty good point, the wind speed is a variable, within a range, and the gear box can be set up to never exceed a certain speed, with the knowledge of automatic transmissions it can even select gears for the conditions without over revving. transformers, to my limited experience simply multiply voltage, while some have multiple settings they are not suitable to constant adjustments. thus the transformer only multiplies the variable range from not enough to dangerously high. while breakers of some form could prevent dangerous conditions, the entire grid becomes dependant on those breakers working. in contrast an over revved gear box only destroys one turbine.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
Don't forget that comercial turbines also have variable pitch blades (and slewing heads) allowing them to match their characteristics to the current wind - direction and strength - that is also another form of gearing (or rather altering the torque the wind provides to the generator )- it is "lossy" outside optimal wind conditions, but some power is better than none.

The mechanical gearing, you may find is more efficient than high ratio power tranforming.

wind effects on a very large diameter generator house can be significant - Vestas has a series of direct drive generators - lave a look at them, also look at their power capacity??

 - a quick quote: "The amount of rare earth elements used in direct-drive turbines is up to 10 times higher than the amounts used in conventional drivetrains"  pros and cons, GE also has some Direct Drive units...  (Inverters come into the mix to output the desired power..)

author: @tichrontus
text: Nowadays we have the more advanced option of CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) for areas where flexibility in conversion ratio is needed. It's found commonly in newer vehicles where it replaces conventional gearboxes. Not sure if there's anyone doing it but I believe it's a good fit for wind turbines where varying speed is a constant issue. A CVT if properly tuned, it can optimize the power curve over a range of speeds. Only trouble is the wear and tear of the belt. Something heavy duty enough to handle large wind turbines probably is not available yet. For small ones, probably a diy CVT will turn out to be more cost effective.

replies.author: @nicholascook9584
replies.text: I was about to make a similar comment and decided to check if it was already made

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: Won't a charge controller on a turbine give you essentially the same result? It adjusts the load to your output to give you optimum efficiency. I'm a noob so I'm not sure, but that's how I thought it worked.

replies.author: @tichrontus
replies.text: @DKFX1 A charge controller only has effects on the electrical portion of the transferred power downstream of the alternator. Whereas the CVT reduces the power loss on the mechanical portion. If my guess is not wrong, you're referencing from photovoltaic systems. If this is the case, photovoltaics revolve around semiconductors, which do not have mechanical components that contribute to power conversion, except when it involves a solar tracker. Then that'll be a completely different issue altogether. However, it MIGHT be ppssible to tackle it from an electrical perspective. The power curve generally is dependant on the alternator used. Having an alternator with a lower output simply means minimum input torque is lower and of course higher for ones with higher outputs. Changing the alternator actually shifts and reshapes the power curve. Within an alternator, it's the strength of the magnets and number of coils that affect not only the power generation but also the mechanical load which corresponds to the minimum torque thus power curve. Therefore, if you have a hybrid alternator with permanent magnets and reinforced with electromagnets that are windings around the permanent magnets, you'll have a fixed and variable component to the magnetic field strength. Combining that with a feedback system to control the variable magnetic field, you can technically tackle the power curve issue which is supposed to be a mechanical problem from an electrical approach instead.

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: @tichrontus I was gonna say the same thing about the field strength. If you are able to change the field strength of the poles, like is possible in an externally excited generator, the torque required to spin the generator up is reduced. Once the generator is up to operating speed or the voltage gets to high, the field strength can be increased which should provide more amps, cause a higher back-EMF and slow the RPM and thus the voltage down. Basically it's the electro/magneto-mechanical version of a CVT but without the friction losses.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Gearing is nothing more than playing with ratios. You can gear up speed by say 5 times but the torque will be 1/5. The power has not changed one bit (ignoring friction losses that is) on the output. In my genator many years ago I created the gearing synthetically by having many magnets (60 in fact) around the device circumference resulting in a slow mechanical speed with a high frequency / speed of magnetic action.  At 1 rev per second which is very slow for my wind mill I was getting 60 hertz. Using a standard transformer action which has a high efficiency (98% or more ) I could get any voltage I wanted without introducing inefficient gearing losses. BTW gears in mechanical terms are very similar to transformers in electrical engineering. You can up the voltage but ĺose the current.  The total power is still the same on the output (assuming you ignore copper losses and eddy current losses in the transformer).
